title: कुबेर पञ्चरत्नम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री कुबेर पञ्चरत्नम् ॥

धनदं निधिपं यक्षराजं कुबेरमीश्वरम्।
शिवसखं गुह्यकाधीशं प्रणमामि धनप्रदम्॥ १॥

अलकापुरनाथं च नरवाहनसंस्थितम्।
रत्नगर्भसमोपेतं प्रणमामि निधीश्वरम्॥ २॥

त्रिलोकधनदातारं सर्वसम्पत्प्रदायकम्।
नवनिधिसमायुक्तं प्रणमामि गदाधरम्॥ ३॥

लक्ष्मीसहोदरं देवं दिक्पालमुत्तरेश्वरम्।
हिमालयवासिनं शान्तं प्रणमामि कुबेरकम्॥ ४॥

सर्वदारिद्र्यनाशाय सर्वसम्पत्समृद्धये।
भक्तानां धनदातारं प्रणमामि कुबेरकम्॥ ५॥

॥ इति श्री कुबेर पञ्चरत्नम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Kubera Pancharatnam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Kubera in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Kubera festivals and daily worship
description: Kubera Pancharatnam (कुबेर पञ्चरत्नम्) is five gems of devotional verses dedicated to Lord Kubera. This sacred composition is sourced from Vishnu Purana and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to grant divine protection and auspiciousness and remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. It is also said to invoke blessings of Kubera in all forms. It is especially recommended on Friday (Shukravar) and Thursday (Guruvar) and during Diwali and Dhanteras for maximum spiritual benefit. The text contains 5 verses and takes around 2 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Kubera Pancharatnam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Kubera Pancharatnam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kubera Pancharatnam (कुबेर पञ्चरत्नम्) is a five-verse (pancha = five, ratna = gem) Sanskrit devotional hymn to Lord Kubera — the celestial treasurer (Dhanapati), king of the yakshas (Yaksharaja), guardian of the northern direction, and lord of Alakapuri on Mount Kailasa. The pancharatnam (five-jewels) is a classical Sanskrit stotra format pioneered by Adi Shankaracharya in compositions like the Ganesha Pancharatnam, Subrahmanya Pancharatnam and Shiva Pancharatnam — five concise, jewel-like verses, each invoking a particular cluster of the deity's attributes and culminating in the formula pranamami (I bow). Each of the five verses describes Kubera in a different aspect: as Dhanada / Yaksharaja / Shiva-sakha (verse 1), as Alakapati and rider of nara-vahana (verse 2), as bestower of all sampat with the nine nidhis (verse 3), as brother of Lakshmi and northern dik-pala (verse 4), and as destroyer of all daridrya (verse 5). (Source: preserved in classical tradition; Vishnu Purana Kubera-stuti corpus.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Lord Kubera and what makes him the supreme deva of wealth?
seoFaqs.answer: Kubera is the Hindu deva of wealth, originally born as the son of sage Vishrava (hence the epithet Vaishravana) and Ilavila (hence Ailavila), making him the half-brother of Ravana, Kumbhakarna and Vibhishana. Through severe tapasya he obtained from Brahma the lordship of all wealth, the Pushpaka Vimana (which Ravana later seized), the rulership of the yaksha and guhyaka clans, and the city of Alakapuri on Mount Kailasa adjoining Shiva's residence — making him Shankara-sakha (friend of Shiva) and the closest deva-companion of Shiva and Parvati. He is enumerated among the eight Lokapalas / Dik-palas as the guardian of the northern direction (Uttara-dik-pala). His mount is the nara-vahana (a man, sometimes shown as a procession of yakshas carrying his palanquin). He keeps the nine nidhis (treasures) — Padma, Mahapadma, Shankha, Makara, Kachchhapa, Mukunda, Kunda, Nila, Kharva — under his stewardship. (Sources: Vishnu Purana; Mahabharata Sabha Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the classical pancharatnam stotra format and how does this hymn follow it?
seoFaqs.answer: The pancharatnam (five-jewels) is one of the most beloved compact stotra formats in Sanskrit devotional literature, popularised by Adi Shankaracharya in his many pancharatnams (Ganesha, Subrahmanya, Shivaparadha-Kshamapana variants, Mahishasura-Mardini stuti related compositions) and adopted across all sampradayas. The format prescribes exactly five verses, each typically in shardulavikridita, sragdhara or anushtup metre, each verse invoking a distinct cluster of the deity's attributes and ending in a salutation formula (pranamami, namami, vande). Five is significant as the number of pancha-tattva (five elements), pancha-mahabhuta, pancha-kosha and pancha-prana — making the pancharatnam a complete invocation. The Kubera Pancharatnam follows this format exactly with five verses, each ending in pranamami (verse 1: pranamami dhanapradam; verse 2: pranamami nidhishvaram; verse 3: pranamami gadadharam; verse 4: pranamami kuberakam; verse 5: pranamami kuberakam). (Source: Adi Shankaracharya pancharatnam tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Kubera Pancharatnam be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kubera Pancharatnam is most powerfully recited on Friday (Shukravar — the day of Shukra and of Lakshmi) and Thursday (Guruvar — the day of Brihaspati and of wealth-deva worship), and on the supreme wealth-festivals: Dhanteras (Kartik Krishna Trayodashi — when Kubera is principally worshipped alongside Dhanvantari), Diwali / Lakshmi Puja (Amavasya night when Lakshmi-Kubera puja is conducted at the household tijori or safe), Akshaya Tritiya (Vaishakha Shukla Tritiya — the day of inexhaustible wealth and the traditional day for buying gold), Kojagari Purnima (Sharad Purnima — sacred to Lakshmi), the start of any new financial year or business venture, and on the day of taking or repaying a major loan. The hymn is also recited daily by businessmen, traders, and householders facing financial difficulties, ideally facing north (Kubera's direction) at pratahkala (early morning) before opening the business or starting work. (Sources: Drikpanchang wealth festivals; Lakshmi-Kubera puja vidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What does it mean that Kubera is Lakshmi-sahodara (brother of Lakshmi)?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 4 of the Kubera Pancharatnam describes Kubera as Lakshmi-sahodaram devam (the deva who is the brother of Lakshmi). This relationship is significant because it establishes the inseparable link between the two principal wealth-deities of Hinduism: Lakshmi is the source-power (shakti) of all prosperity, beauty and auspiciousness; Kubera is the appointed treasurer who keeps, accumulates and distributes the wealth that flows from Lakshmi. They are worshipped together at every Diwali Lakshmi-Kubera puja precisely because Lakshmi's chanchala (fickle, mobile) nature is balanced by Kubera's sthira (stable, accumulating) nature — Lakshmi brings wealth in, Kubera makes it stay. The sahodara (same-womb-born) imagery is a poetic device emphasising their inseparability rather than literal mythology — both are emanations of the divine artha-shakti. The classical wealth formula is therefore: invoke Lakshmi for arrival, invoke Kubera for retention. (Source: Lakshmi-Kubera puja tradition; classical artha-shastra.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Kubera Pancharatnam grant according to tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition (the phalashruti context of verse 5 — Sarva-daridrya-nashaya, sarva-sampat-samriddhaye, bhaktanam dhana-dataram pranamami Kuberakam) attributes the following blessings to sincere recitation of the Kubera Pancharatnam: (1) Sarva-daridrya-nasha — destruction of every form of poverty (financial, intellectual, spiritual); (2) Sarva-sampat-samriddhi — abundance of every kind of prosperity (dhana wealth, dhanya grain, putra progeny, suvarna gold, ratna gems, vidya knowledge, kirti fame); (3) blessing of the nine nidhis (treasures) coming into the devotee's household; (4) protection of the northern direction of one's house and business (since Kubera is uttara-dik-pala — many vastu traditions place the safe / cash-counter in the northern part of the building); (5) success in business and trade (Kubera being the deva of vanijya, commerce); (6) clearance of debts; (7) divine protection through the friendship of Shiva (since Kubera is Shankara-sakha). (Source: Kubera Pancharatnam phalashruti.)
